methodology: Rankings reflect group-stage and knockout results through 12 July 2026, weighted by results quality, goal difference, squad availability and path difficulty. Title win chances are illustrative estimates aligned with Soccer Stats Hub modelling.

rankings.summary: France remain the benchmark after Kylian Mbappe's eighth goal of the tournament and Ousmane Dembele's fifth settled a 2-0 quarter-final win over Morocco in Foxborough. Didier Deschamps should have Aurelien Tchouameni back for the Dallas semi-final against Spain, though William Saliba and Dayot Upamecano were managed in training, and Les Bleus are targeting a third consecutive World Cup final.

Spain reached their first World Cup semi-final since 2010 when Mikel Merino scored an 88th-minute winner against Belgium, conceding their first goal of the tournament to Charles De Ketelaere. Lamine Yamal has beaten France in their last two tournament meetings and Luis de la Fuente's side arrive in Dallas unbeaten in 37 competitive matches, but La Roja have needed late goals in both knockout ties.

Argentina needed Julian Alvarez's 112th-minute screamer and a Lautaro Martinez stoppage-time third to beat 10-man Switzerland 3-1 in Kansas City, their second extra-time knockout win of the tournament. Lionel Messi's eight goals keep him level with Mbappe in the Golden Boot race, but Lionel Scaloni's holders have now flirted with elimination in three straight knockouts before Wednesday's Atlanta semi-final with England.

England reached their first World Cup semi-final since 2018 as Jude Bellingham scored twice in extra time to beat Norway 2-1 in Miami, though Thomas Tuchel said the performance was sloppy and lucky. Declan Rice is recovering from illness, Jarell Quansah remains suspended, and the Three Lions face Argentina in Atlanta with both quarter-finalists having played 120 minutes in the Florida heat.

Norway's first World Cup quarter-final since 1998 ended in Miami when Jude Bellingham equalised in first-half stoppage time and punished Orjan Nyland's spilled save in extra time. Erling Haaland was subdued and substituted in the second period of extra time, closing a run that had included a last-16 brace to eliminate Brazil.

Belgium matched Spain for long periods in Los Angeles and became the first team to score against La Roja when Charles De Ketelaere headed in, only for Mikel Merino to convert a rebound in the 88th minute. Rudi Garcia's side leave after their best tournament performance ended one late goal short of a first semi-final since 1986.

Morocco lost 2-0 to France in Foxborough despite Yassine Bounou saving Kylian Mbappe's first-half penalty, with Mohamed Ouahbi's striker-less lineup failing to threaten Les Bleus. The Atlas Lions exit at the same stage as 2022, when they also lost 2-0 to France in a World Cup semi-final.

Switzerland matched their best World Cup run since 1954 but fell 3-1 to Argentina in extra time after Breel Embolo's controversial second yellow for simulation. Dan Ndoye had equalised before the red card and Gregor Kobel kept Argentina at bay for nearly 50 minutes with 10 men until Julian Alvarez's long-range winner.

Brazil leave after Erling Haaland's late double punished Bruno Guimaraes' missed penalty and a string of wasted chances in East Rutherford. Carlo Ancelotti's side exit before the quarter-finals for the first time since 1990 despite dominating possession, ending an eight-tournament run of last-eight appearances.

Portugal bowed out 1-0 to Spain in Dallas after Mikel Merino's stoppage-time winner, with Cristiano Ronaldo's World Cup career likely over at 41. Roberto Martinez's side created little after the first half and leave with an expected-goals figure of 0.60 against Spain's 1.77.

Mexico's co-host run ended with a 3-2 defeat to England at the Estadio Azteca, ending a ten-match unbeaten home record at the tournament's most iconic venue. Javier Aguirre's side fought back after Jarell Quansah's red card and Raul Jimenez's late penalty, but could not find an equaliser in 11 minutes of stoppage time.

The United States were the last co-host eliminated after a 4-1 defeat to Belgium in Seattle, with Matt Freese's error outside his box gifting Hans Vanaken a third goal. Mauricio Pochettino's side exit at the last 16 for the fourth consecutive World Cup they have played, despite Malik Tillman's deflected free-kick briefly levelling the score.

Colombia matched their best World Cup run since 2014 but fell 4-3 on penalties to Switzerland after a goalless 120 minutes in Vancouver. Davinson Sanchez sent his penalty over the bar and Gregor Kobel saved Cucho Hernandez's effort, ending a campaign that had conceded only once in four matches.

Egypt led Argentina 2-0 in Atlanta and were on the cusp of their greatest World Cup result before Lionel Messi, Cristian Romero and Enzo Fernandez scored in the final 13 minutes. Hossam Hassan decried VAR decisions after the match, but the Pharaohs still leave having reached the last 16 for the first time in their history.

Paraguay leave after making France uncomfortable in a physical 1-0 defeat under extreme Philadelphia heat. The Germany upset remains one of the tournament's defining shocks, but the lack of attacking volume eventually caught up with them against Mbappe.

Canada's co-host run ended with a 3-0 defeat to Morocco, despite early chances for Jonathan David and Tani Oluwaseyi. The scoreline was harsh on the first-half performance, but Morocco punished the spaces Canada left once they chased the game.

Australia were knocked out by Egypt on penalties after a 1-1 draw in Dallas. Mat Ryan was introduced for the shoot-out but could not save any of Egypt's four kicks, while Harry Souttar and Lucas Herrington missed for the Socceroos.

Cape Verde produced one of the tournament's great performances in defeat, twice equalising against Argentina before a 111th-minute own goal ended their run. Sidny Lopes Cabral's extra-time equaliser and Vozinha's eight saves will keep them near the top of the tournament story list.

Ghana's tournament ended in a 1-0 defeat to Colombia, with Jhon Arias scoring early and Ghana unable to create enough pressure to recover. Their draw with England remains the high point, but the knockout attack never found the same threat.

DR Congo pushed England to the brink in Atlanta before Harry Kane's late double sealed a 2-1 win for the Three Lions. Brian Cipenga's early strike and Lionel Mpasi's heroics in goal showed Sebastien Desabre's side belonged at this stage, but their first knockout campaign ended one comeback short.

Senegal led Belgium 2-0 with five minutes remaining through Habib Diarra and Ismaila Sarr before Romelu Lukaku and Youri Tielemans forced extra time, and a controversial VAR-awarded penalty in the 125th minute ended their tournament in Seattle. Aliou Cisse's side played without Edouard Mendy and will rue one of the most painful collapses in World Cup knockout history.

Luka Modric's fifth World Cup ended in Toronto when Portugal came from behind to win 2-1, with Josko Gvardiol's stoppage-time equaliser disallowed by VAR after a connected-ball review. Ivan Perisic had given Croatia the lead in a match featuring two 40-year-old outfielders, but Zlatko Dalic's ageing squad could not hold on.

Ralf Rangnick's Austria reached their first World Cup knockout stage since 1954 but were comprehensively beaten 3-0 by Spain at SoFi Stadium on 2 July. Alexander Schlager made six saves to keep the score respectable, but Austria managed just one real chance as substitute Sasa Kalajdzic headed over.

Algeria held Argentina to a draw in the group stage but were beaten 2-0 by Switzerland in Vancouver on 2 July, ending their wait for a first World Cup knockout victory. Vahid Halilhodzic's side had more possession but managed just 0.73 expected goals and rarely threatened after Dan Ndoye doubled the Swiss lead seconds after half-time.

Bosnia reached the knockouts for the first time after beating Qatar 3-1, with Edin Dzeko still the focal point at 40. A Round of 32 tie with co-hosts USA at Levi's Stadium ended in a 2-0 defeat after Folarin Balogun and Malik Tillman scored, closing a historic campaign that included a draw with Canada.

Sebastian Beccacece's side stunned Germany 2-1 in the group stage but could not repeat the trick at the Estadio Azteca, losing 2-0 to Mexico on 1 July. Piero Hincapie's stoppage-time red card compounded a miserable evening for a team that had already shown they can trouble elite opposition.

Sweden advanced as a best third-placed side but were comprehensively beaten 3-0 by France at MetLife Stadium on 30 June, conceding seven goals across their four tournament matches. Graham Potter admitted it was no disgrace to lose to this France team, but the defensive frailties exposed against the Netherlands returned in New Jersey.

The Elephants reached their first World Cup knockout stage and fought back through Amad Diallo's equaliser in Dallas, only for Erling Haaland to break their hearts with an 86th-minute winner. Franck Kessie and Sebastien Haller provided experience, but Norway's ruthlessness ended Ivory Coast's best run in a generation.
